title: Kubernetes HPA with Custom Metrics
description: Configure Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to scale applications based on custom application metrics exposed via Prometheus and the Prometheus Adapter. You will deploy a sample app that exposes custom metrics, set up the metrics pipeline, and verify autoscaling driven by application-specific signals.
prerequisites: Running Kubernetes cluster (minikube, kind, or cloud-managed)
kubectl configured and connected to the cluster
Basic understanding of Kubernetes Deployments, Services, and HPA
Familiarity with Prometheus concepts (scraping, metrics, PromQL)
learningObjectives: Deploy Prometheus and the Prometheus Adapter into a Kubernetes cluster
Instrument an application to expose custom Prometheus metrics
Configure the Prometheus Adapter to register custom metrics with the Kubernetes API
Create an HPA that scales based on a custom application metric
Debug and verify the custom metrics pipeline end-to-end

steps.title: Create a Namespace and Verify Cluster Readiness
steps.description: Before installing any components, create a dedicated namespace for the exercise and verify that your cluster is healthy. Isolating resources in a namespace keeps things organized and makes cleanup straightforward.
steps.expectedOutput: Namespace 'hpa-custom-metrics' created. Cluster info shows a healthy control plane and all nodes are in Ready status.
validationCriteria: Namespace hpa-custom-metrics exists
All cluster nodes are in Ready state

steps.title: Install Prometheus via Helm
steps.description: Deploy the kube-prometheus-stack using Helm. Prometheus is the backbone of the custom metrics pipeline — it scrapes metrics from your application pods and stores them. The Prometheus Adapter (installed in the next step) will query Prometheus and expose those metrics to the Kubernetes custom metrics API so that HPA can consume them.
steps.expectedOutput: The kube-prometheus-stack Helm release is deployed. All pods in the hpa-custom-metrics namespace reach Running status within a few minutes.
hints: If pods are stuck in Pending, check node resources with kubectl describe node
Use --wait to ensure Helm blocks until all resources are healthy
Setting serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues=false allows Prometheus to pick up ServiceMonitors from any namespace
validationCriteria: Helm release 'prometheus' is listed in 'helm list -n hpa-custom-metrics'
Prometheus server pod is Running
Alertmanager and Grafana pods are Running

steps.title: Deploy a Sample Application with Custom Metrics
steps.description: Deploy a sample HTTP application that exposes a custom Prometheus metric called http_requests_total on its /metrics endpoint. This counter increments with every request the app receives. HPA will later use the per-second rate of this metric to make scaling decisions.
steps.codeExample:
# sample-app.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: sample-app
  namespace: hpa-custom-metrics
  labels:
    app: sample-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: sample-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: sample-app
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "8080"
        prometheus.io/path: "/metrics"
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: sample-app
        image: luxas/autoscale-demo:v0.1.2
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 64Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 200m
            memory: 128Mi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: sample-app
  namespace: hpa-custom-metrics
  labels:
    app: sample-app
spec:
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
  selector:
    app: sample-app
  type: ClusterIP
steps.expectedOutput: Deployment sample-app is available with 1 running pod.
hints: Verify the /metrics endpoint works by port-forwarding: kubectl port-forward svc/sample-app 8080:80 -n hpa-custom-metrics, then curl http://localhost:8080/metrics
Look for the http_requests_total metric in the output
validationCriteria: sample-app Deployment has 1 ready replica
Pod is in Running status
Service sample-app exists and targets port 8080
curl to /metrics endpoint returns http_requests_total

steps.title: Create a ServiceMonitor for Prometheus Scraping
steps.description: A ServiceMonitor tells Prometheus how to discover and scrape your application's metrics endpoint. Without it, Prometheus will not collect your custom metrics and the adapter will have nothing to serve. This is the glue between your application and the metrics pipeline.
steps.codeExample:
# service-monitor.yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: sample-app
  namespace: hpa-custom-metrics
  labels:
    app: sample-app
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: sample-app
  endpoints:
  - port: http
    interval: 15s
    path: /metrics
steps.expectedOutput: ServiceMonitor sample-app is created in the hpa-custom-metrics namespace.
hints: After creating the ServiceMonitor, wait 30-60 seconds for Prometheus to pick it up
You can verify Prometheus is scraping the target by port-forwarding to the Prometheus UI (port 9090) and checking Status > Targets
validationCriteria: ServiceMonitor resource exists
Prometheus Targets page shows sample-app as an active scrape target
Querying http_requests_total in Prometheus returns data

steps.title: Install and Configure the Prometheus Adapter
steps.description: The Prometheus Adapter bridges Prometheus and the Kubernetes custom metrics API (custom.metrics.k8s.io). It runs PromQL queries against Prometheus and translates the results into the format that HPA expects. You will configure a rule that exposes the per-second rate of http_requests_total as a custom metric called http_requests_per_second.
steps.codeExample:
# prometheus-adapter-values.yaml
prometheus:
  url: http://prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.hpa-custom-metrics.svc
  port: 9090
rules:
  custom:
  - seriesQuery: 'http_requests_total{namespace!="",pod!=""}'
    resources:
      overrides:
        namespace: {resource: "namespace"}
        pod: {resource: "pod"}
    name:
      matches: "^(.*)_total$"
      as: "${1}_per_second"
    metricsQuery: 'sum(rate(<<.Series>>{<<.LabelMatchers>>}[2m])) by (<<.GroupBy>>)'
commands: helm install prometheus-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter --namespace hpa-custom-metrics --values prometheus-adapter-values.yaml --wait --timeout 3m
steps.expectedOutput: Prometheus Adapter pod is Running. The adapter is configured with the custom rule to expose http_requests_per_second.
hints: The Prometheus URL must match the actual service name — check with: kubectl get svc -n hpa-custom-metrics | grep prometheus
If the adapter pod CrashLoops, inspect logs: kubectl logs -n hpa-custom-metrics -l app.kubernetes.io/name=prometheus-adapter
The metricsQuery template uses <<.Series>>, <<.LabelMatchers>>, and <<.GroupBy>> placeholders that the adapter fills in at query time
validationCriteria: Prometheus Adapter pod is Running
Helm release prometheus-adapter is listed
Adapter logs show no connection errors to Prometheus

steps.title: Verify Custom Metrics Are Available via the API
steps.description: Before creating the HPA, confirm that the custom metrics pipeline is working end-to-end. Query the Kubernetes custom metrics API to ensure http_requests_per_second is registered and returning values. If this step fails, HPA will not be able to scale on the custom metric.
steps.expectedOutput: The first command lists all available custom metrics. The second command returns metric values for the sample-app pod, with a non-zero value for http_requests_per_second.
hints: If the custom metrics API returns a 404, ensure the Prometheus Adapter is running and its APIService is registered: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.custom.metrics.k8s.io

If the metric is not listed, generate a few requests first: kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it --restart=Never -n hpa-custom-metrics -- curl http://sample-app.hpa-custom-metrics.svc/
It can take 1-2 minutes after initial requests for the rate query to produce non-zero values
validationCriteria: custom.metrics.k8s.io API is available
http_requests_per_second metric appears in the metric list
Querying the metric for the sample-app pod returns a numeric value

steps.title: Create the HPA with Custom Metrics
steps.description: Now create the HPA that scales the sample-app based on the custom http_requests_per_second metric. The target is set to 10 requests per second per pod — when the average exceeds this threshold, HPA will add more pods to distribute the load.
steps.codeExample:
# hpa-custom-metrics.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: sample-app-hpa
  namespace: hpa-custom-metrics
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: sample-app
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: http_requests_per_second
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "10"
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 30
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 2
        periodSeconds: 30
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 120
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 25
        periodSeconds: 60
steps.expectedOutput: HPA is created and shows the current value of http_requests_per_second alongside the target of 10. TARGETS column should display a real value (e.g., 0/10 or 500m/10) rather than <unknown>/10.
hints: If TARGETS shows <unknown>, the custom metrics API is not returning data — revisit the previous step
The 'type: Pods' metric means HPA averages the metric value across all pods in the Deployment
AverageValue of 10 means HPA targets 10 requests/second per pod
validationCriteria: HPA sample-app-hpa exists in the namespace
HPA references the sample-app Deployment
TARGETS column shows a numeric value, not <unknown>
Min/Max replicas are 1 and 10 respectively

steps.title: Generate Load to Trigger Scale-Up
steps.description: Deploy a load generator that sends a high volume of HTTP requests to the sample-app service. This will push the http_requests_per_second metric above the HPA target of 10, triggering a scale-up event. Watch the HPA and pod count as the system responds.
steps.codeExample:
# load-generator.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: load-generator
  namespace: hpa-custom-metrics
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: load-generator
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: load-generator
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: load-generator
        image: busybox:1.36
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - |
          while true; do
            for i in $(seq 1 50); do
              wget -q -O- http://sample-app.hpa-custom-metrics.svc/ > /dev/null 2>&1 &
            done
            sleep 0.5
          done
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 50m
            memory: 32Mi
commands: echo 'Waiting 90 seconds for load to build and HPA to react...'
steps.expectedOutput: After 60-90 seconds, http_requests_per_second should exceed the target of 10. HPA increases the replica count, and you should see multiple sample-app pods.
hints: Monitor in real time with: kubectl get hpa sample-app-hpa -n hpa-custom-metrics -w
It typically takes 1-2 minutes for the metric rate to spike and HPA to make a scaling decision
If no scale-up occurs, check the metric value: kubectl get --raw '/apis/custom.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/hpa-custom-metrics/pods/*/http_requests_per_second' | python3 -m json.tool
validationCriteria: Load generator is running
http_requests_per_second exceeds the target value of 10
HPA has scaled the Deployment to more than 1 replica
New sample-app pods are in Running state

steps.title: Verify Scaling and Observe Metrics
steps.description: Confirm that the autoscaling is working correctly by examining HPA events, checking that load is distributed across pods, and verifying the custom metric values per pod. This step validates the entire pipeline from application metrics through Prometheus to HPA decisions.
commands: kubectl get pods -n hpa-custom-metrics -l app=sample-app -o wide
steps.expectedOutput: HPA describe shows scaling events. Events list SuccessfulRescale entries. Multiple sample-app pods are running. The per-pod metric value should be lower now that load is distributed.
validationCriteria: HPA events show at least one SuccessfulRescale event
Multiple sample-app pods are running and ready
Per-pod http_requests_per_second is closer to the target value now that load is distributed
No error conditions in HPA status

steps.title: Remove Load and Clean Up Resources
steps.description: Delete the load generator and observe the scale-down behavior. Once the request rate drops below the target, HPA will gradually reduce the replica count back to the minimum. After verifying scale-down, clean up all resources created during this exercise.
commands: echo 'Load removed. Waiting 3 minutes for scale-down...'
steps.expectedOutput: After removing load, the metric drops and HPA scales the Deployment back to 1 replica. All Helm releases and the namespace are deleted.
hints: Scale-down respects the stabilizationWindowSeconds (120s) configured in the HPA behavior section
If you want to keep the setup for further experimentation, skip the cleanup commands
validationCriteria: Load generator is deleted
HPA scales back to minReplicas (1)
All Helm releases are uninstalled
Namespace hpa-custom-metrics is deleted

completionCriteria: Prometheus is scraping custom application metrics via a ServiceMonitor
Prometheus Adapter exposes http_requests_per_second through the custom.metrics.k8s.io API
HPA successfully scales pods up when the custom metric exceeds the target
HPA scales pods back down when load is removed
The full metrics pipeline (app -> Prometheus -> Adapter -> HPA) is verified end-to-end

troubleshooting.issue: HPA shows <unknown> in the TARGETS column for the custom metric
troubleshooting.solution: Verify the custom metrics API is working: kubectl get --raw '/apis/custom.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1'. If it returns a 404, check the Prometheus Adapter pod logs and ensure the APIService is registered: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.custom.metrics.k8s.io

Check the adapter logs: kubectl logs -n hpa-custom-metrics -l app.kubernetes.io/name=prometheus-adapter. The most common cause is an incorrect Prometheus URL in the values file. Verify the URL matches the actual Prometheus service: kubectl get svc -n hpa-custom-metrics | grep prometheus

troubleshooting.issue: Custom metric returns zero even after generating load
troubleshooting.solution: The rate() function needs at least two data points. Wait 2-3 minutes after starting the load generator. Also verify Prometheus is actually scraping the app by checking the Targets page in the Prometheus UI (port-forward to port 9090).

troubleshooting.issue: ServiceMonitor is created but Prometheus is not scraping the app
troubleshooting.solution: Ensure the Prometheus operator is configured to pick up ServiceMonitors from the namespace. Check that the ServiceMonitor labels match and that Prometheus was installed with serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues=false.

troubleshooting.issue: Helm install fails with timeout or resource quota errors
troubleshooting.solution: The kube-prometheus-stack requires significant resources. Ensure your cluster has at least 4GB of available memory and 2 CPUs. If using minikube, start it with: minikube start --memory=4096 --cpus=2

resources.title: Kubernetes HPA with Custom Metrics Documentation
